Information Sequencing.explanation: This nails exactly how I think about email structure — the four-beat arc is real, and the note about not collapsing the acknowledgment and news into one continuous thought captures something I feel strongly but wouldn't have easily put into words.

Audience-Centric Framing.explanation: Yes — when the 'why it matters' reasoning centers the sender's concerns instead of the reader's actual situation, the email feels tone-deaf to me, and this criterion captures that precisely.

Actionable Specificity.explanation: The core of this is right — one owner, one deadline, no group asks — but the line about not over-specifying org structure details is something I care about and it's here, though I'd say the criterion slightly over-explains it in a way that could itself feel over-specified.

Tonal Discipline.explanation: The emphasis on nothing trailing after the CTA is exactly right — sentimental closings and helpdesk-style redirects after the action item genuinely bother me and weaken the email.

Directness and Transparency.explanation: This matches my dealbreakers around jargon and corporate detachment, and the point about not hedging bad news into ambiguity is something I care about in practice.

Scannability.explanation: Short paragraphs and easy extraction on a single pass are real preferences of mine, though this criterion is the least surprising — it's basically what I said upfront about concise and direct.

q2: The 'rhythmic break' between acknowledgment and news — the idea that they shouldn't flow into each other as one continuous thought — is something I genuinely care about but would never have articulated if asked. I just know when a draft feels rushed or compressed at the top, and this criterion names why. The audience-centric framing criterion also surfaced something I feel but usually just label as 'tone-deaf' without being able to explain the actual structural reason.
